title: Code isnвЂ™t the only thing causing your production summary: Ryan sits down with Anish Agarwal, CEO and co-founder of Traversal, to chat about why AI coding agents have made writing code easier but running it safely in production harder, why production failures are really caused by interactions between systems and not just the code itself, and how teams can troubleshoot more effectively when traditional observability tools are not enough for agentic AI...

sections.overview: Ryan sits down with Anish Agarwal, CEO and co-founder of Traversal, to chat about why AI coding agents have made writing code easier but running it safely in production harder, why production failures are really caused by interactions between systems and not just the code itself, and how teams can troubleshoot more effectively when traditional observability tools are not enough for agentic AI...
sections.technical_breakdown: Ryan sits down with Anish Agarwal, CEO and co-founder of Traversal, to chat about why AI coding agents have made writing code easier but running it safely in production harder, why production failures are really caused by interactions between systems and not just the code itself, and how teams can troubleshoot more effectively when traditional observability tools are not enough for agentic AI workflows.вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊп»їп»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊп»їп»ївЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»їп»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»їп»ївЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊп»їп»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂЊп»їп»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»їп»ївЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂЊп»їп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»їп»ївЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂЊп»їп»ївЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊп»їп»ївЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»їп»їп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊп»їп»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂЊп»їп»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»їп»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»їв email_summary: Ryan sits down with Anish Agarwal, CEO and co-founder of Traversal, to chat about why AI coding agents have made writing code easier but running it safely in production harder, why production failures are really caused by interactions between systems and not just the code itself, and how teams can troubleshoot more effectively when traditional observability tools are not enough for agentic AIвЂ¦

title: Build a RHEL environment with image builder and content templates
summary: Consistent image builds, or golden images are an important part of any standard operating environment (SOE). You need to guarantee that the software in the image remains consistent from build to build until you update your approved package set. Beyond the image, you need to be sure that your running systems are receiving the same updates as your image, so that you can maintain consistency...
timeline_entries: Step 1: Create a content template To create a content template, visit the Templates page in the Hybrid Cloud Console and click the Create template button. In the creation wizard, select your RHEL operating system version

For our example, choose RHEL 10, x86_64, and Virtualization - Guest image (.qcow2) . Then proceed through the configuration options, setting values that match your needs, such as automatically registering the system with
Proceed through the rest of the steps and choose the Create blueprint and build image(s) option .

sections.overview: Consistent image builds, or golden images are an important part of any standard operating environment (SOE). You need to guarantee that the software in the image remains consistent from build to build until you update your approved package set. Beyond the image, you need to be sure that your running systems are receiving the same updates as your image, so that you can maintain consistency throughout your environment.
sections.technical_breakdown:
It enables you to standardize and streamline your deployments by applying customizations at build time.

We can see the impact of the content template on our running system by logging into the system console and running sudo dnf update , which shows that there are no updates to install. 

 [rhel10-soe@rhel10-soe-vm ~]$ sudo dnf update
Updating Subscription Management repositories.

We can see the impact of the content template on our running system by logging into the system console.
sections.impact:
The 17 applicable advisories are updates that impact this system, but published after March 31. We can see the impact of the content template on our running system by logging into the system console and running sudo dnf update , which shows that there are no updates to install. 

 [rhel10-soe@rhel10-soe-vm ~]$ sudo dnf update
Updating Subscription Management repositories. We can see the impact of the content template on our running system by logging into the system console.

Classified impact band: medium. Affected layer signal: general production.
sections.resolution:
Dependencies resolved.

Updates Information Summary: available
 10 Security notice(s)
 1 Critical Security notice(s)
 6 Important Security notice(s)
 3 Moderate Security notice(s)
 3 Bugfix notice(s)
[rhel10-soe@rhel10-soe-vm ~]$ Any new system you deploy for your rebuilt image and any existing system you patch will include the same package versions, resulting in consistent and predictable operations across your environment.
sections.lessons_learned:
For this example, we will build and deploy a RHEL 10 for x86_64 system with the packages and updates published through March 31, 2026.

This time we will run sudo dnf updateinfo , which shows that there are 13 updates to install, including 10 security advisories. 

 [rhel10-soe@rhel10-soe-vm ~]$ sudo dnf updateinfo
Updating Subscription Management repositories.
sections.root_cause_highlight: Likely root-cause theme: deployment failure. It enables you to standardize and streamline your deployments by applying customizations at build time. Because we embedded an activation key in our image and built it from a content template, our system automatically registers to the Hybrid Cloud Console with the content template applied.

For our example, choose RHEL 10, x86_64, and Virtualization - Guest image (.qcow2) . Then proceed through the configuration options, setting values that match your needs, such as automatically registering the system with
Proceed through the rest of the steps and choose the Create blueprint and build image(s) option .

title: Implement mTLS and zero trust with cert-manager and trust-manager
summary: We are excited to announce significant upgrades to the cert-manager operator for Red Hat OpenShift . We recently released version 1.19 of the operator, which contains an exciting new feature: the technology preview of trust-manager. The trust-manager controller is an optional add-on feature, enabling administrators to customize and distribute bundles of trusted certificate authorities (CAs) to...
timeline_entries: We are excited to announce significant upgrades to the cert-manager operator for Red Hat OpenShift . We recently released version 1.19 of the operator, which contains an exciting new feature: the technology preview of tr

First, to facilitate a secure TLS handshake, we require two artifacts: the CA bundle and the TLS certificate. In an enterprise environment, the CA bundle serves as the trust anchor. It is a collection of public key certi

Success : The client and server confirm the other certificate is valid, and the encrypted session is established.

sections.overview: We are excited to announce significant upgrades to the cert-manager operator for Red Hat OpenShift . We recently released version 1.19 of the operator, which contains an exciting new feature: the technology preview of trust-manager. The trust-manager controller is an optional add-on feature, enabling administrators to customize and distribute bundles of trusted certificate authorities (CAs) to workloads across Kubernetes clusters.
sections.technical_breakdown:
The most common form of network communication is TLS, a methodology found in web browsers or in many protocols that use HTTPS, such as API calls.

If you would like to explore a deeper understanding of TLS or mTLS, this Cloudflare blog is a good start.

In the simple example above, the server pod in namespace A receives a TLS certificate minted by the intermediate CA in the cert-manager namespace, and the client pod in namespace B receives the Bundle from trust-manager including the public keys of the intermediate and root CAs so that when it communicates with the server pod, it can verify that the TLS certificate supplied was properly signed by both CAs.

Each running workload (i.e., pod) gets a signed TLS certificate.

Pod A in namespace A get a TLS certificate signed by intermediate CA - A, and Pod B gets a TLS certificate signed by intermediate CA - B 
 From here, the mTLS handshake proceeds as described in the previous introductory section.
sections.impact:
The client terminates the connection; or in the case of some web browsers, warns the user that it does not trust the server s certificate, and the user should proceed with caution. This bundle provides the reference needed for a client (and server, in mTLS scenarios) to verify that a presented certificate is authentic. For TLS, the following occurs: 

 The client (e.g., web browser) initiates communication. The server (e.g., backend web server) responds to the client with a TLS certificate.

Classified impact band: medium. Affected layer signal: platform вЂ” kubernetes.
sections.resolution: Resolution detail may be partial in the RSS excerpt. Refer to the original write-up for timelines, mitigations, and verification steps.
sections.lessons_learned:
TLS vs mTLS To highlight the benefits of trust-manager, we will give a little background about transport layer security (TLS) and its more secure iteration, mutual transport layer security (mTLS).

This introduces a significant improvement to the security of an organization as a whole as a breach to the OpenShift cluster will never expose the Root certificate authority private key, which is used as the highest level of authority for minting TLS certificates.
sections.root_cause_highlight: Likely root-cause theme: dns issue. This requirement breaks down for any non-public DNS use case with internal domains such as cluster.internal.corp . Previously, it was much more difficult to rotate CA certificates at shorter intervals because this added additional manual tasks of reconstructing and distributing new trust bundles.

Success : The client and server confirm the other certificate is valid, and the encrypted session is established.

The critical first step here is that trust-manager benefits from cert-manager s ability to connect to almost any external PKI. This means that an enterprise can wire up cert-manager to connect to their PKI of choice. Cer

title: Scaling without friction: Aliases at project scope in Boundary
summary: In Boundary, aВ targetВ alias is a simple, powerful tool. It lets you connect to infrastructure using a memorable name instead of a random, generated ID. It gives developers something intuitive to work with.В But those aliases lived exclusively at the global scope. They must be globally unique across your entire deployment.В
timeline_entries: Download the latest version ofВ  Boundary installer В to try it out yourself.

sections.overview: In Boundary, aВ targetВ alias is a simple, powerful tool. It lets you connect to infrastructure using a memorable name instead of a random, generated ID. It gives developers something intuitive to work with.
sections.technical_breakdown:
Aliases are now automatically constructed as:В <alias>.<project-suffix>.<org-suffix>В 

 By introducing structured suffixes with aliases at both the project and organization levels, we are bringing a natural, DNS-style hierarchyВ mimicking the Boundary deployment structureВ directly to your access paths.

Real-worldВ example:В globalВ enterpriseВ deployment В  Consider a large enterprise structuring Boundary across geographic regions and business departments.

They must be globally unique across your entire deployment.

Teams can now independently create, manage, and use aliases within their own project boundaries,В aligned with how they already map their deployment structures.

EveryВ networkВ project needs an ssh-jumper.
sections.impact:
ConfiguringВ grants В  To support this new workflow, two new actions are introduced that will enable users with project admin and org admin roles to create or remove suffixes at project scope and orgВ scopesВ respectively. But those aliases lived exclusively at the global scope. With our 1.0 release , Boundary introducesВ targetВ aliases at theВ projectВ scopeВ level. It'sВ simple:В 

 Define an alias onceВ 

 EnsureВ it'sВ uniqueВ 

 Use it with any target under any scope in BoundaryВ 

 В 

 But at enterprise scale, this model starts to break down.

Classified impact band: medium. Affected layer signal: data store.
sections.resolution:
IntroducingВ structuredВ suffixes В  The answer is not more rules.

Aliases are now automatically constructed as:В <alias>.<project-suffix>.<org-suffix>В 

 By introducing structured suffixes with aliases at both the project and organization levels, we are bringing a natural, DNS-style hierarchyВ mimicking the Boundary deployment structureВ directly to your access paths.

This suffix model mirrors that structure in names, making connections more intuitive.

Reduced namespace collisions : Org suffixes scope project suffix uniqueness within each org.
sections.lessons_learned:
It'sВ better structure.

Better deployment structure : The hierarchy aligns with enterprise org structures, creating clearer namespace ownership and governance boundaries.
sections.root_cause_highlight:
Likely root-cause theme: deployment failure. They must be globally unique across your entire deployment. Teams can now independently create, manage, and use aliases within their own project boundaries,В aligned with how they already map their deployment structures. Aliases are now automatically constructed as:В <alias>.<project-suffix>.<org-suffix>В 

 By introducing structured suffixes with aliases at both the project and organization levels, we are bringing a natural, DNS-style hierarchyВ mimicking the Boundary deployment structureВ directly to your access paths.

title: HCP Vault Dedicated introduces cluster disaster recovery (public preview)
summary: HCP Vault Dedicated is a fully managed secrets management solution that organizations rely on for authentication, dynamic secrets, and encryption workflows. As a core piece of security infrastructure, the cost of downtime is too high to leave recovery to chance. HCP Vault Dedicated already supports regional disaster recovery (DR) to protect against large-scale infrastructure outages...
timeline_entries: Today, weвЂ™re introducing cluster disaster recovery (cluster DR) in public preview, available as a support-enabled feature for HCP Vault Dedicated customers. This capability adds a critical new layer of resilience byВ enab

Cluster DR enables operational resilience Cluster DR allows teams to fail over an individual Vault cluster even when the primary region remains available. During the public preview, the feature is available for productio

Support security response workflows When teams detect a potential compromise, recovery actions often need to happen quickly. Cluster DR enables them to isolate the affected cluster, promote the DR secondary cluster, and
Combining cluster DR with regional DR Teams achieve the strongest resilience by combining both recovery strategies.В Regional DR protects where Vault runs.В Cluster DR protects how Vault operates.

sections.overview: HCP Vault Dedicated is a fully managed secrets management solution that organizations rely on for authentication, dynamic secrets, and encryption workflows. As a core piece of security infrastructure, the cost of downtime is too high to leave recovery to chance. HCP Vault Dedicated already supports regional disaster recovery (DR) to protect against large-scale infrastructure outages, butВ cluster-specific incidents require a different kind of readiness.
sections.technical_breakdown:
Extending recovery beyond regional failures Regional DR protects against failures like cloud provider outages, regional networking disruptions, and large-scale infrastructure incidents.

HCP Vault Dedicated already supports regional disaster recovery (DR) to protect against large-scale infrastructure outages, butВ cluster-specific incidents require a different kind of readiness.

Cluster DR enables operational resilience Cluster DR allows teams to fail over an individual Vault cluster even when the primary region remains available.

This provides a recovery option for cluster-level security events that regional DR alone was not designed to address.

Supporting enterprise hybrid cloud operations Enterprises that operate across hybrid and multi-cloud environments depend on Vault as a critical control plane for security and application delivery.
sections.impact:
HCP Vault Dedicated already supports regional disaster recovery (DR) to protect against large-scale infrastructure outages, butВ cluster-specific incidents require a different kind of readiness. Extending recovery beyond regional failures Regional DR protects against failures like cloud provider outages, regional networking disruptions, and large-scale infrastructure incidents. Together, these approaches ensure that teams can respond effectively to infrastructure outages and application-level failures without compromising availability. Prepare for a broader range of failure scenariosВ  As enterprises scale their use of Vault, they must address the reality that most disruptions originate fromВ software, operational, or security issues, not full regional outages.

Classified impact band: critical. Affected layer signal: networking.
sections.resolution:
This capability adds a critical new layer of resilience byВ enabling cluster-level failover and DR testing, soВ teams canВ rehearse incident response before real outages occur.

This lets teams intentionally test failover behavior for Vault-cluster-specific scenarios and verify runbooks, recovery coordination, and service continuity under controlled conditions.

Customers can request failover and failback through HashiCorp support.

To request a failover test, submit a support ticket with:

 В·В В В В В В Cluster details (name, organization ID)

 В·В В В В В В Failover window (within 16 hours)

 The Vault team will support the failover and failback process.
sections.lessons_learned: Lessons are not explicit in the excerpt; use the source discussion for durable engineering takeaways.
sections.root_cause_highlight: Likely root-cause theme: control plane issue. This lets teams intentionally test failover behavior for Vault-cluster-specific scenarios and verify runbooks, recovery coordination, and service continuity under controlled conditions. Cluster DR enables them to isolate the affected cluster, promote the DR secondary cluster, and restore service continuity in a controlled environment. Supporting enterprise hybrid cloud operations Enterprises that operate across hybrid and multi-cloud environments depend on Vault as a critical control plane for security and application delivery.
sections.incident_timeline:
Today, weвЂ™re introducing cluster disaster recovery (cluster DR) in public preview, available as a support-enabled feature for HCP Vault Dedicated customers. This capability adds a critical new layer of resilience byВ enab
Extending recovery beyond regional failures Regional DR protects against failures like cloud provider outages, regional networking disruptions, and large-scale infrastructure incidents. However, it still assumes that the
Cluster DR enables operational resilience Cluster DR allows teams to fail over an individual Vault cluster even when the primary region remains available. During the public preview, the feature is available for productio
Support security response workflows When teams detect a potential compromise, recovery actions often need to happen quickly. Cluster DR enables them to isolate the affected cluster, promote the DR secondary cluster, and 
Combining cluster DR with regional DR Teams achieve the strongest resilience by combining both recovery strategies.В Regional DR protects where Vault runs.В Cluster DR protects how Vault operates.
To request a failover test, submit a support ticket with:

title: Why you should use Fromager to build your Python dependency trees from source
summary: If you've ever tried to rebuild an entire Python dependency tree from source, you know it's not a simple process. Packages depend on other packages, which have build-time dependencies, which also have their own dependencies, and so on. It's easy to get the order wrong, which eventually causes the build to fail. Fromager solves this problem with two key artifacts: build-order.json and graph.json ...
timeline_entries: But there are already projects and tools that build Python wheels, so the obvious question is why we need Fromager. In this blog post, we delve into the motivations behind Fromager and explain its unique role in the ecos

Also there's no way to customize build flags per package. You can set global environment variables but you can't, for instance, specify to build numpy with CUDA support and scipy with OpenBLAS. There's no patching mechan

Common alternatives have similar issues. The uv tool has the same limitations as pip , and conda doesn't build from source at all. Bazel can auto-generate targets for PyPI dependencies, but primarily consumes pre-built w

fromager bootstrap stevedore This single command kicks off a multi-step orchestration: 1. Resolves stevedore to a specific version 2. Discovers that stevedore needs setuptools and pbr to build 3. Discovers that stevedore

The graph reveals several distinct edge types, such as top-level, install, and build-system. These categories are significant because they define the specific nature of each dependency relationship. The stevedore module
Install dependencies: Packages needed to use the software at runtime ( numpy , requests ). These are discovered after the build phase completes, by reading metadata from the resulting wheel or sdist.

Fromager customizable without forking This is one of the biggest strengths of Fromager. When you are building hundreds of packages, you inevitably come across situations where the Python project doesn't follow the standa

Stage 1 (the discovery phase) resolves versions, queries package indexes, and runs PEP 517 hooks. Because those hooks are arbitrary Python code defined by each package's build backend, this stage executes untrusted upstr
